(Italian: [[ˈbreʃʃa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Brescia.ogg "File:It-Brescia.ogg"), locally [[ˈbreːʃa]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"); [Brescian](/wiki/Eastern_Lombard "Eastern Lombard"): *Brèsa* [[ˈbrɛsɔ, ˈbrɛhɔ, ˈbrɛsa, ˈbrɛha]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Lombard "Help:IPA/Lombard"); [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language"): *Bressa* or *Bresa*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Brixia*) is a city and *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* (municipality) in the region of [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"), in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). It is situated at the foot of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), a few kilometers from the lakes [Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda") and [Iseo](/wiki/Lake_Iseo "Lake Iseo"). With a population of 201,279, it is the second largest city in Lombardy, the fourth largest in [northwest Italy](/wiki/Northwest_Italy "Northwest Italy") and it is the northernmost Italian city among those with over 200,000 inhabitants. The urban area of Brescia extends beyond the administrative city limits and has a population of 672,822,[[3]](#cite_note-UrbanismiInItalia-3) while over 1.5 million people live in its [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area").[[3]](#cite_note-UrbanismiInItalia-3) The city is the administrative capital of the [Province of Brescia](/wiki/Province_of_Brescia "Province of Brescia"), one of the largest in Italy, with over 1.2 million inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

**Brescia** (Italian: [[ˈbreʃʃa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Brescia.ogg "File:It-Brescia.ogg"), locally [[ˈbreːʃa]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"); [Brescian](/wiki/Eastern_Lombard "Eastern Lombard"): *Brèsa* [[ˈbrɛsɔ, ˈbrɛhɔ, ˈbrɛsa, ˈbrɛha]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Lombard "Help:IPA/Lombard"); [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language"): *Bressa* or *Bresa*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Brixia*) is a city and *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* (municipality) in the region of [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"), in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). It is situated at the foot of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), a few kilometers from the lakes [Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda") and [Iseo](/wiki/Lake_Iseo "Lake Iseo"). With a population of 201,279, it is the second largest city in Lombardy, the fourth largest in [northwest Italy](/wiki/Northwest_Italy "Northwest Italy") and it is the northernmost Italian city among those with over 200,000 inhabitants. The urban area of Brescia extends beyond the administrative city limits and has a population of 672,822,[[3]](#cite_note-UrbanismiInItalia-3) while over 1.5 million people live in its [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area").[[3]](#cite_note-UrbanismiInItalia-3) The city is the administrative capital of the [Province of Brescia](/wiki/Province_of_Brescia "Province of Brescia"), one of the largest in Italy, with over 1.2 million inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

Founded over 3,200 years ago, Brescia (in antiquity Brixia) has been an important regional centre since pre-Roman times. Its old town contains the best-preserved [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") public buildings in northern Italy[[4]](#cite_note-benic-4)[[5]](#cite_note-unesc-5) and numerous monuments, such as the [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") castle, the [Old](/wiki/Old_Cathedral,_Brescia "Old Cathedral, Brescia") and [New cathedral](/wiki/New_Cathedral,_Brescia "New Cathedral, Brescia"), the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") *[Piazza della Loggia](/wiki/Piazza_della_Loggia "Piazza della Loggia")* and the [rationalist](/wiki/Rationalism_(architecture) "Rationalism (architecture)") *Piazza della Vittoria*.

The monumental archaeological area of the Roman forum and the [monastic complex of San Salvatore-Santa Giulia](/wiki/San_Salvatore,_Brescia "San Salvatore, Brescia") have become a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") as part of a group of seven inscribed as [Longobards in Italy, Places of Power](/wiki/Longobards_in_Italy,_Places_of_Power_(568%E2%80%93774_A.D.) "Longobards in Italy, Places of Power (568–774 A.D.)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Brescia is considered to be an important industrial city.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Metallurgy and production of metal parts, machine tools and firearms are of particular economic significance, along with mechanical and automotive engineering. Major companies based in the Brescia area include utility company [A2A](/wiki/A2A "A2A"), steel producer [Lucchini](/wiki/Gruppo_Lucchini "Gruppo Lucchini") and firearms manufacturers [Beretta](/wiki/Beretta "Beretta") and [Perazzi](/wiki/Perazzi "Perazzi").

Brescia is home to the prestigious [Mille Miglia](/wiki/Mille_Miglia "Mille Miglia") classic car race that starts and ends in the town.

In the arts, it was nicknamed *Leonessa d'Italia* ("The Lioness of Italy"). [Gabriele d'Annunzio](/wiki/Gabriele_d%27Annunzio "Gabriele d'Annunzio") selected [Gardone Riviera](/wiki/Gardone_Riviera "Gardone Riviera") (nearby on the shores of [Garda Lake](/wiki/Garda_Lake "Garda Lake")) as his final residence. The estate he built (largely thanks to state-sponsored funding), il [Vittoriale](/wiki/Vittoriale_degli_Italiani "Vittoriale degli Italiani"), is now a public institution devoted to the arts; a museum dedicated to him is hosted in his former residence. Brescia is also the setting for most of the action in [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni")'s 1822 play *[Adelchi](/wiki/Adelchi "Adelchi")*.

The province is known for being the production area of the [Franciacorta](/wiki/Franciacorta_DOCG "Franciacorta DOCG") sparkling wine, as well as the main source of Italian-produced [caviar](/wiki/Caviar "Caviar"). Brescia with her territory was the "European Region of Gastronomy" in 2017 and the "Italian Capital of Culture" with [Bergamo](/wiki/Bergamo "Bergamo") in 2023.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Brescia](/wiki/Timeline_of_Brescia "Timeline of Brescia").

### Ancient era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Parco_archeologico_di_Brescia_romana_Vittoria_alata_Brescia.jpg/250px-Parco_archeologico_di_Brescia_romana_Vittoria_alata_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parco_archeologico_di_Brescia_romana_Vittoria_alata_Brescia.jpg)

*Winged Victory of Brescia* (1st century [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"))[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Various myths relate to the founding of Brescia: one assigns it to [Hercules](/wiki/Hercules "Hercules"), while another attributes its foundation as *Altilia* ("the other Ilium") by a fugitive from the siege of [Troy](/wiki/Troy "Troy"). According to another myth, the founder was the king of the [Ligures](/wiki/Ligures "Ligures"), [Cidnus](/wiki/Cycnus_(son_of_Sthenelus) "Cycnus (son of Sthenelus)"), who had invaded the [Padan Plain](/wiki/Padan_Plain "Padan Plain") in the late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"). *Colle Cidneo* (Cidnus's Hill) was named after that version, and it is the site of the medieval castle. This myth seems to have a grain of truth, because recent archaeological excavations have unearthed remains of a settlement dating back to 1,200 BC that scholars presume to have been built and inhabited by Ligures peoples.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Others scholars[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] attribute[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] the founding of Brescia to the [Etruscans](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Gallic](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls") [Cenomani](/wiki/Cenomani_(Cisalpine_Gaul) "Cenomani (Cisalpine Gaul)"), allies of the [Insubres](/wiki/Insubres "Insubres"), invaded in the 7th century BC, and used the town as their capital. The city became Roman in 225 BC, when the Cenomani submitted to the Romans. During the [Carthaginian Wars](/wiki/Carthaginian_Wars "Carthaginian Wars"), 'Brixia' (as it was called then) was allied with the Romans. During a Celtic alliance against [Rome](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") the city remained faithful to the Romans. With their Roman allies the city attacked and destroyed the Insubres by surprise. Subsequently, the city and the tribe entered the Roman world peacefully as faithful allies, maintaining a certain administrative freedom. In 89 BC, Brixia was recognized as *civitas* ("city"), and in 41 BC, 48 years later, its inhabitants finally received Roman citizenship. [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") founded a civil (not military) colony there in 27 BC, and he and [Tiberius](/wiki/Tiberius "Tiberius") constructed an [aqueduct](/wiki/Roman_aqueduct "Roman aqueduct") to supply it. Roman Brixia had at least three temples, an aqueduct, a theatre, a forum with another temple built under [Vespasianus](/wiki/Vespasianus "Vespasianus"), and some baths.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

When [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") advanced against [Maxentius](/wiki/Maxentius "Maxentius") in AD 312, an engagement took place at Brixia in which the enemy was forced to retreat as far as [Verona](/wiki/Verona "Verona"). In 402, the city was ravaged by the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") of [Alaric I](/wiki/Alaric_I "Alaric I"). During the 452 invasion of the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") under [Attila](/wiki/Attila "Attila"), the city was besieged and sacked. Forty years later, it was one of the first conquests by the Gothic general [Theoderic the Great](/wiki/Theoderic_the_Great "Theoderic the Great") in his war against [Odoacer](/wiki/Odoacer "Odoacer").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Brescia_-_Castello.jpg/250px-Brescia_-_Castello.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brescia_-_Castello.jpg)

The medieval castle of Brescia

In 568 (or 569), Brescia was taken from the [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") by the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards"), who made it the capital of one of their semi-independent duchies. The first duke was Alachis, who died in 573. Later dukes included the future kings of the Lombards [Rothari](/wiki/Rothari "Rothari") and [Rodoald](/wiki/Rodoald "Rodoald"), and Alachis II, a fervent anti-Catholic, who was killed in battle at [Cornate d'Adda](/wiki/Cornate_d%27Adda "Cornate d'Adda") in 688. The last king of the Lombards, [Desiderius](/wiki/Desiderius "Desiderius"), also held the title Duke of Brescia.

In 774, [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") captured the city and ended the presence of the Lombard kingdom in northern Italy. Notingus was the first (prince-)bishop (in 844) who bore the title of [count](/wiki/Count "Count") (see [Bishopric of Brescia](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Brescia "Bishopric of Brescia")). From 855 to 875, under [Louis II the Younger](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Italy "Louis II of Italy"), Brescia became *de facto* capital of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). Later the power of the bishop as imperial representative was gradually opposed by the local citizens and nobles, resulting in Brescia becoming a [free commune](/wiki/Free_commune "Free commune") around the early 12th century. Subsequently, it expanded into the nearby countryside, first at the expense of the local landholders, and later against the neighbouring communes, notably [Bergamo](/wiki/Bergamo "Bergamo") and [Cremona](/wiki/Cremona "Cremona"). Brescia defeated the latter twice at [Pontoglio](/wiki/Pontoglio "Pontoglio"), then at the Grumore (mid-12th century) and in the battle of the Malamorte (Bad Death) (1192).

In 1138, Brescia experienced a communal revolt against the local Bishop Manfred led by radical reformer and [Canons regular](/wiki/Canons_regular "Canons regular") [Arnold of Brescia](/wiki/Arnold_of_Brescia "Arnold of Brescia").[[12]](#cite_note-12) This revolt broke out due to the city's involvement in the ecclesiastical and political conflict that resulted from the [1130 papal election](/wiki/1130_papal_election "1130 papal election"). This controversial election divided the [College of Cardinals](/wiki/College_of_Cardinals "College of Cardinals") and caused a schism between [Pope Innocent II](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_II "Pope Innocent II") (who had the minority vote) and [Antipope Anacletus II](/wiki/Antipope_Anacletus_II "Antipope Anacletus II") (who had the majority vote). During the early 1130s, when Anacletus had power over Brescia, he appointed Bishop Villanus to the diocese, but in 1132 Innocent regained control and installed Manfred. Despite Manfred supporting the reformed clergy, which Brescia had historical supported with its proximity to [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and the [Pataria](/wiki/Pataria "Pataria") reform movement in the 11th century, Manfred was cast out as he clashed with the growth of the commune and the local nobility.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) The revolt began around 1135 and was manageable at first, but by 1138 Manfred was forced to seek papal support and left for Rome. Arnold is believed to have joined the revolt around this time, as contemporary historian [John of Salisbury](/wiki/John_of_Salisbury "John of Salisbury") records that Arnold only "so swayed the minds of the citizens that they would scarcely open their gates to the bishop on his return."[[15]](#cite_note-15) Manfred was therefore forced to return to Rome and was likely witness to the [Second Council of the Lateran](/wiki/Second_Council_of_the_Lateran "Second Council of the Lateran") in 1139, after which he obtained Pope Innocent's support and had Arnold exiled from Italy. Arnold's home was Brescia, but he would never return to the city; instead he developed his reform ideology while in exile and continued to dissent against the Church. He worked with intellectual [Peter Abelard](/wiki/Peter_Abelard "Peter Abelard") (who he potentially studied under in the 1110s) who was condemned of heresy at the [Council of Sens](/wiki/Councils_of_Sens "Councils of Sens") 1141 and went on to join the [Commune of Rome](/wiki/Commune_of_Rome "Commune of Rome") in 1148, which led to his execution by [Frederick Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_Barbarossa "Frederick Barbarossa") and [Pope Adrian IV](/wiki/Pope_Adrian_IV "Pope Adrian IV") in 1155.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Torre_della_Pallata_lato_ovest_Brescia.jpg/250px-Torre_della_Pallata_lato_ovest_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torre_della_Pallata_lato_ovest_Brescia.jpg)

The [Pallata Tower](/wiki/Pallata_Tower,_Brescia "Pallata Tower, Brescia")

During the struggles of the 12th and 13th centuries between the Lombard cities and the Holy Roman emperors, Brescia was implicated either in league with the emperors or against them. In the [Battle of Legnano](/wiki/Battle_of_Legnano "Battle of Legnano") the contingent from Brescia was second in size to that of [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"). The [Peace of Constance](/wiki/Peace_of_Constance "Peace of Constance") (1183) that ended the war with [Frederick Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_Barbarossa "Frederick Barbarossa") confirmed officially the free status of the *comune*. In 1201 the *[podestà](/wiki/Podest%C3%A0 "Podestà")* [Rambertino Buvalelli](/wiki/Rambertino_Buvalelli "Rambertino Buvalelli") made peace and established a league with Cremona, Bergamo, and [Mantua](/wiki/Mantua "Mantua"). Memorable also was the [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Brescia_(1238) "Siege of Brescia (1238)") laid by the Emperor [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor") in 1238 on account of the part taken by Brescia in the [Battle of Cortenova](/wiki/Battle_of_Cortenova "Battle of Cortenova") (1237). Brescia came through this assault victorious. After the fall of the [Hohenstaufen](/wiki/Hohenstaufen "Hohenstaufen"), republican institutions declined in Brescia as in the other free cities and the leadership was contested between powerful families, chief among them the Maggi and the Brusati, the latter of the (pro-imperial, anti-papal) [Ghibelline](/wiki/Ghibelline "Ghibelline") party. In 1258 the city fell into the hands of [Ezzelino da Romano](/wiki/Ezzelino_III_da_Romano "Ezzelino III da Romano").

In 1311 [Emperor Henry VII](/wiki/Emperor_Henry_VII "Emperor Henry VII") laid siege to Brescia for six months, losing three-fourths of his army. Later the [Scaliger](/wiki/Scaliger "Scaliger") of Verona, aided by the exiled Ghibellines, sought to place Brescia under subjugation. The citizens of Brescia then had recourse to [John of Luxemburg](/wiki/John_I_of_Bohemia "John I of Bohemia"), but [Mastino II della Scala](/wiki/Mastino_II_della_Scala "Mastino II della Scala") expelled the governor appointed by him. His mastery was soon contested by the [Visconti of Milan](/wiki/Visconti_of_Milan "Visconti of Milan"), but not even their rule was undisputed, as [Pandolfo III Malatesta](/wiki/Pandolfo_III_Malatesta "Pandolfo III Malatesta") took possession of the city in 1406. However, in 1416 he bartered it to [Filippo Maria Visconti](/wiki/Filippo_Maria_Visconti "Filippo Maria Visconti") duke of Milan, who in 1426 sold it to the Venetians. The Milanese nobles forced Filippo to resume hostilities against the Venetians, and thus to attempt the recovery of Brescia, but he was defeated in the [Battle of Maclodio](/wiki/Battle_of_Maclodio "Battle of Maclodio") (1427), near Brescia, by general [Carmagnola](/wiki/Francesco_Bussone_da_Carmagnola "Francesco Bussone da Carmagnola"), commander of the Venetian mercenary army. In 1439, Brescia was once more besieged by [Francesco Sforza](/wiki/Francesco_Sforza "Francesco Sforza"), captain of the Venetians, who defeated [Niccolò Piccinino](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Piccinino "Niccolò Piccinino"), Filippo's [condottiero](/wiki/Condottiero "Condottiero"). Thenceforward Brescia and the province were a Venetian possession, only disrupted by the French conquest in 1512.

### Early Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early Modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Mortier%2C_Pierre_%281661-1711%29%2C_Mappa_di_Brescia_a_inizio_Settecento.jpg/250px-Mortier%2C_Pierre_%281661-1711%29%2C_Mappa_di_Brescia_a_inizio_Settecento.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mortier,_Pierre_(1661-1711),_Mappa_di_Brescia_a_inizio_Settecento.jpg)

Map of Brescia in the early 18th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Broletto_cortile_e_cupola_duomo_nuovo_Brescia.jpg/250px-Broletto_cortile_e_cupola_duomo_nuovo_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Broletto_cortile_e_cupola_duomo_nuovo_Brescia.jpg)

The dome of the New Cathedral

Brescia has had a major role in the [history of the violin](/wiki/History_of_the_violin "History of the violin"). Many archive documents very clearly testify that from 1490 to 1640 Brescia was the cradle of a magnificent school of string players and makers, all styled "maestro", of all the different kinds of stringed instruments of the Renaissance: [viola da gamba](/wiki/Viola_da_gamba "Viola da gamba") (viols); [violone](/wiki/Violone "Violone"); [lyra](/wiki/Lyra "Lyra"); [lyrone](/wiki/Lyrone "Lyrone"); [violetta](/wiki/Violetta_(instrument) "Violetta (instrument)"); and [viola da brazzo](/w/index.php?title=Viola_da_brazzo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Viola da brazzo (page does not exist)"). So you can find from 1495 "maestro delle viole" or "maestro delle lire" and later, at least from 1558, "maestro di far violini" that is master of violin making. From 1530 the word violin appeared in Brescian documents and spread in later decades throughout north of Italy, reaching Venezia and Cremona.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Early in the 16th century, Brescia was one of the wealthiest cities of Lombardy, but it never recovered from [its sack by the French](/wiki/List_of_battles_of_the_Italian_Wars#Brescia "List of battles of the Italian Wars") in 1512.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The "Sack of Brescia" took place on 18 February 1512, during the [War of the League of Cambrai](/wiki/War_of_the_League_of_Cambrai "War of the League of Cambrai"). The city of Brescia had revolted against French control, garrisoning itself with [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") troops. [Gaston de Foix](/wiki/Gaston_of_Foix,_Duke_of_Nemours "Gaston of Foix, Duke of Nemours"), recently arrived to command the French armies in Italy, ordered the city to surrender; when it refused, he attacked it with around 12,000 men. The French attack took place in a pouring rain, through a field of mud; Foix ordered his men to remove their shoes for better traction.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The defenders inflicted heavy casualties on the French, but were eventually overrun, suffering 8,000 – 15,000 casualties.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [Gascon](/wiki/Gascony "Gascony") infantry and [landsknechts](/wiki/Landsknechts "Landsknechts") then proceeded to thoroughly sack the city, massacring thousands of civilians over the next five days. Following this, the city of [Bergamo](/wiki/Bergamo "Bergamo") paid some 60,000 [ducats](/wiki/Ducat "Ducat") to the French to avoid a similar fate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The French occupied Brescia until 1520, when Venetian rule resumed. Thereafter, Brescia shared the fortunes of the Venetian republic [until the latter fell](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Republic_of_Venice "Fall of the Republic of Venice") at the hands of French general [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1769, in the [Brescia explosion](/wiki/Brescia_explosion "Brescia explosion"), the city was devastated when the Bastion of San Nazaro was struck by [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning"). The resulting fire ignited 90,000 kg (90 t; 200,000 lb; 99 short tons) of [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") stored there, causing a massive explosion which destroyed one-sixth of the Brescia and killed 3,000 people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1799, during the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), the fortress, occupied by French troops, fell to the advancing allies of the [Second Coalition](/wiki/Second_Coalition "Second Coalition") (see [Capture of Brescia](/wiki/Capture_of_Brescia "Capture of Brescia")).

### 19th century and later

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th century and later")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Piazza_della_Vittoria_Brescia.jpg/250px-Piazza_della_Vittoria_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piazza_della_Vittoria_Brescia.jpg)

Piazza della Vittoria, example of Italian [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism_(architecture) "Rationalism (architecture)"), built between 1927 and 1932 by the architect [Marcello Piacentini](/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini "Marcello Piacentini")

In the [Napoleonic era](/wiki/Napoleonic_era "Napoleonic era"), Brescia was part of the various [revolutionary republics](/wiki/Revolutionary_republics "Revolutionary republics") and then of the [Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Napoleonic_Kingdom_of_Italy "Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy") after Napoleon became Emperor of the French. After the end of the Napoleonic era in 1815, Brescia was annexed to the Austrian puppet state known as the [Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lombardy%E2%80%93Venetia "Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia").

Brescia revolted in [1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_Italian_states "Revolutions of 1848 in the Italian states"); then again in March 1849, when the Piedmontese army invaded Austrian-controlled Lombardy, the people in Brescia overthrew the hated local Austrian administration, and the Austrian military contingent, led by General [Julius Jacob von Haynau](/wiki/Julius_Jacob_von_Haynau "Julius Jacob von Haynau"), retreated to the [Castello di Brescia](/wiki/Castello_di_Brescia "Castello di Brescia") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castello_di_Brescia "it:Castello di Brescia")]. When the larger military operations turned against the Piedmontese, forcing them to retreat, Brescia was left to its own resources. Still, the citizens managed to resist recapture by the Austrian army for ten days of bloody and obstinate street fighting that are now celebrated as the [Ten Days of Brescia](/wiki/Ten_Days_of_Brescia "Ten Days of Brescia"). This prompted poet [Giosuè Carducci](/wiki/Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci "Giosuè Carducci") to nickname Brescia *Leonessa d'Italia* ("Lioness of Italy") for it fierce resistance.

In 1859, the city was conquered by the Italian troops and Brescia was included in the newly founded [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(1861%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Italy (1861–1946)").

The city was awarded a gold medal for its resistance against Fascism in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

On 28 May 1974, it was the seat of the bloody [Piazza della Loggia bombing](/wiki/Piazza_della_Loggia_bombing "Piazza della Loggia bombing").

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Topography")]

Brescia is located in the northwestern section of the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley"), at the foot of the [Brescian Prealps](/wiki/Brescia_and_Garda_Prealps "Brescia and Garda Prealps"), between the [Mella](/wiki/Mella_(river) "Mella (river)") and the Naviglio, with the [Lake Iseo](/wiki/Lake_Iseo "Lake Iseo") to the west and the [Lake Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda") to the east (but it has also other important lakes like Idro and Moro[[19]](#cite_note-19)). The southern area of the city is flat, while towards the north the territory becomes hilly. The city's lowest point is 104 metres (341 ft) above sea level, the highest point is [Monte Maddalena](/wiki/Monte_Maddalena "Monte Maddalena") at 874 metres (2,867 ft), while the centre of the town is 149 metres (489 ft). The administrative comune covers a total area of 90.3 square kilometres (34.9 mi2).

Modern Brescia has a central area focused on residential and tertiary activities. Around the city proper, lies a vast urban agglomeration with over 600,000 inhabitants that expands mainly to the north, to the west and to the east, engulfing many communes in a continuous urban landscape.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Brescia has a mid-latitude [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (***Cfa***). Its average annual temperature is 13.7 °C (57 °F): 18.2 °C (65 °F) during the day and 9.1 °C (48 °F) at night. The warmest months are June, July, and August, with high temperatures from 27.8 to 30.3 °C (82 to 87 °F). The coldest are December, January, and February, with low temperatures from −1.5 to 0.6 °C (29 to 33 °F).

Winter is moderately cold, but not harsh, with some snow, mainly occurs from December through February, but snow cover does not usually remain for long. Summer can be sultry, when humidity levels are high and peak temperatures can reach 35 °C (95 °F). Spring and autumn are generally pleasant, with temperatures ranging between 10 and 20 °C (50 and 68 °F).

The relative humidity is high throughout the year, especially in winter when it causes fog, mainly from dusk until late morning, although the phenomenon has become increasingly less frequent in recent years.

Precipitation is spread evenly throughout the year. The driest month is December, with precipitation of 54.6 mm (2.15 in), while the wettest month is May, with 104.9 mm (4.13 in) of rain.

As of 2025, Brescia has a population of 199,949, of whom 48.4% are male and 51.6% female. Minors (children aged 0–17) totalled 16% of the population compared to pensioners who number 24.6%. This compares with the Italian average of 16.5% (minors) and 22% (pensioners). Between 2024 and 2025, the population of Brescia grew by 0.85%, while Italy as a whole declined by 0.06%.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The current birth rate of Brescia is 7.9 births per 1,000 inhabitants compared to the Italian average of 8 births.

Brescia is one of the most cosmopolitan and multicultural cities in Italy. In 2020, the foreign-born residents represented 18% of the total population.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) The largest immigrant group comes from East European nations (mostly [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova") and [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania")), the others from South Asia (mostly [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")) and Africa. The city is predominantly [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), but due to immigration now has some [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Orthodoxy#Christianity "Orthodoxy"), [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Italy "Sikhism in Italy") and [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") followers.

In 2006 there were about 1,000 people of Pakistani origins living in Brescia.[[28]](#cite_note-PophamInd-28)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Government")]

See also: [List of mayors of Brescia](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Brescia "List of mayors of Brescia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Palazzo_della_Loggia_2021.jpg/250px-Palazzo_della_Loggia_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_della_Loggia_2021.jpg)

Palazzo della Loggia, Brescia City Hall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/415BresciaBroletto.jpg/250px-415BresciaBroletto.jpg)](/wiki/File:415BresciaBroletto.jpg)

Palazzo Broletto, seat of the [Province](/wiki/Province_of_Brescia "Province of Brescia") and of the [Prefecture](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture") of Brescia

Since local government political reorganization in 1993, Brescia has been governed by the City Council of Brescia, which is based in *Palazzo della Loggia*. Voters elect directly 32 councilors and the mayor of Brescia every five years.

Brescia was generally considered in the past one of the most important political [bellwether](/wiki/Bellwether_(politics) "Bellwether (politics)") in Italy. Historical stronghold of [DC](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)") party, in 1994 it was the city in which was firstly experimented the newborn political center-left coalition formed by members of former [PCI](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") and DC parties against [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi")'s center-right coalition: that year the last secretary of [DC](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)") and former minister, [Mino Martinazzoli](/wiki/Mino_Martinazzoli "Mino Martinazzoli"), run as mayor with the support of the leftist [PDS](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_the_Left "Democratic Party of the Left") and won the election defeating the [Forza Italia](/wiki/Forza_Italia_(1994) "Forza Italia (1994)")-[Lega Nord](/wiki/Lega_Nord "Lega Nord") bloc candidate, endorsed by Berlusconi. This experience is considered even today one of the bases of [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi")'s [The Olive Tree](/wiki/The_Olive_Tree_(political_coalition) "The Olive Tree (political coalition)") political coalition.

Since then to 2008 the center-left coalition held the largest number of seats with a partnership administration based on the alliance between the major left-wing, green and independents parties. Anyway, in the 2008 local elections the center-right coalition formed by Silvio Berlusconi's [People of Freedom](/wiki/People_of_Freedom "People of Freedom") party and the regionalist [Lega Nord](/wiki/Lega_Nord "Lega Nord") won for the first time the majority in the City Council. These elections occurred the same day Berlusconi's coalition achieved an outright majority across the country. However, in the 2013 elections the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)") achieved an outright majority across the city and the center-left coalition became again the major force in the City Council. In the 2018 local elections the center-left coalition obtained even the 54% of the votes on the first round and the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)"), which obtained nearly the 35% of the votes, gained 15 seats out of 32 in the City Council. In the 2023 local elections the center-left coalition obtained again the 54% of the votes on the first round.

The current mayor of Brescia is [Laura Castelletti](/wiki/Laura_Castelletti "Laura Castelletti"), a [center-left](/wiki/Center-left_coalition "Center-left coalition") [independent](/wiki/Independent_(politician) "Independent (politician)"), elected on 20 May 2023. She previously served as deputy mayor for 10 years between 2013 and 2023.

Brescia is also the capital of its own province. The Provincial Council is seated in *Palazzo Broletto*.

#### Monumental area of the Roman forum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Monumental area of the Roman forum")]

This is the archaeological complex where there are the best-preserved Roman public buildings in the [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"),[[4]](#cite_note-benic-4)[[5]](#cite_note-unesc-5) composed of:

* *Republican sanctuary*

:   It is under the Capitoline temple. It has been built in the 1st century BC and it is the oldest structure of the [forum](/wiki/Forum_(Roman) "Forum (Roman)"). It consists of four rectangular rooms next to each other and inside them, there are the remains of the original [mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") floors and the wall [frescoes](/wiki/Frescoes "Frescoes"), which from a stylistic point of view and state of preservation are comparable to those of [Pompeii](/wiki/Pompeii "Pompeii").[[29]](#cite_note-arc-29) Since the spring of 2015, the western room has opened to the public, while the rest of the building is still undergoing archaeological excavation and restoration.

* *[Capitolium of Brixia](/wiki/Capitolium_of_Brixia "Capitolium of Brixia")*

:   The primary temple in the city, it was dedicated to the cult of the [Capitoline Triad](/wiki/Capitoline_Triad "Capitoline Triad"). It was built in 73 AD and consists of three *[cellae](/wiki/Cella "Cella")* that have preserved much of the original polychrome [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") floors,[[29]](#cite_note-arc-29) while their interior walls are now a [lapidarium](/wiki/Lapidarium "Lapidarium") displaying ancient Roman [epigraphs](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") collected in the 19th century. In front of the cellae, is a fragmentary [portico](/wiki/Portico "Portico"), composed of [Corinthian](/wiki/Corinthian_order "Corinthian order") columns that support a [pediment](/wiki/Pediment "Pediment") containing a dedication to the [Emperor Vespasian](/wiki/Emperor_Vespasian "Emperor Vespasian"). Almost entirely buried by a landslide of the Cidneo Hill, it was rediscovered in 1823 through various archaeological campaigns. During excavation in 1826, a splendid bronze statue of a [winged Victory](/wiki/Nike_(mythology) "Nike (mythology)") was found inside it, likely hidden in late antiquity to preserve it from pillage. After restoration completed in 2013, the site reopened as a new archaeological park.

* *Roman theatre*

:   It is located immediately at east of the *Capitolium*. It has been built in the [Flavian era](/wiki/Flavian_dynasty "Flavian dynasty") and altered in the 3rd century. With its 86 m (282 ft) diameter, is one of the largest [Roman theatres](/wiki/Roman_theatre_(structure) "Roman theatre (structure)") in northern Italy and originally it housed around 15,000 spectators. In the 5th century, an earthquake has heavily damaged the building. In addition, in later centuries, its remains were incorporated into new buildings built on top of it, largely demolished starting from the 19th century. Of the original structure are preserved the semicircular perimeter walls, the two side passages (*aditus*) and the remains of the *[proscenium](/wiki/Proscenium "Proscenium")*, as well as many fragments of columns and friezes of the *[scaenae frons](/wiki/Scaenae_frons "Scaenae frons")*. Most of the *orchestra* and the *ima [cavea](/wiki/Cavea "Cavea")* are still below ground. The archaeological excavations should resume in the coming years.

Near the *Capitolium* is located the *Palazzo Maggi Gambara*, an aristocratic palace built in the 16th century on top of the west ruins of the Roman theatre.

The monastic complex of San Salvatore-Santa Giulia is an outstanding architectural palimpsest,[[4]](#cite_note-benic-4)[[30]](#cite_note-30) today transformed into the *Museo di Santa Giulia*, which contains about 11,000 works of art and archaeological finds.[[31]](#cite_note-31) During the period of Longobard domination, Princess [Anselperga](/wiki/Anselperga "Anselperga"), daughter of King [Desiderius](/wiki/Desiderius "Desiderius"), headed the monastery. It consists of:

* *Basilica of San Salvatore*

:   It has been built in 753 by Duke of Brescia Desiderius, future Lombard king, and his wife Ansa. It is characterized by the simultaneous use of the Longobards stylistic elements and decorative motifs of classical and Byzantine art and it is one of the most important examples of High Middle Ages architecture in Italy.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The basilica has a nave with two apses and has a transept with three apses. It is located over a pre-existing church, which had a single nave and three apses. Expanded in the following centuries, it houses various works of art, including the *Stories of St. Obizio* painted by [Romanino](/wiki/Romanino "Romanino") and *Stories of the Virgin and the infancy of Christ* by Paolo Caylina il Giovane,[[33]](#cite_note-monastic-33) as well as others from the Carolingian age.

* *Church of Santa Maria in Solario*

:   It has been built in the mid-12th century as a chapel inside the monastery. It has a square base with an octagonal lantern and has two internal levels.[[33]](#cite_note-monastic-33) Four vaults, supported in the centre by an ancient Roman altar, covers the lower floor, while a hemispherical dome covers the upper chamber, that has, into the east wall, three small apses. Inside there are frescoes by [Floriano Ferramola](/wiki/Floriano_Ferramola "Floriano Ferramola") and two of the most important pieces of the treasure of the ancient monastery: the [Brescia Casket](/wiki/Brescia_Casket "Brescia Casket") (that consists of a small ivory box dating the 4th century) and the Cross of Desiderius (made of silver and gold plate, studded with 212 precious gems).[[34]](#cite_note-34)

* *The nuns' choir*

:   It is placed between the Basilica of San Salvatore and the church of Santa Giulia. It has been built between the late 15th and early 16th century and it is on two levels. The lower level is the old churchyard covered for access to the basilica. The upper floor is the real choir, made up by a room covered by a barrel vault, which is connected to the east with San Salvatore by three small windows with a grating, on the west by Santa Giulia through an arch. The interior of the choir is entirely decorated with frescoes painted by Ferramola and Caylina, and inside are shown different funerary monuments of the Venetian age, including the *[Martinengo Mausoleum](/wiki/Martinengo_Mausoleum "Martinengo Mausoleum")*, a masterpiece of the Renaissance sculpture in Lombardy.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

* *Church of Santa Giulia*

:   It has been built between 1593 and 1599. The façade, made of Botticino marble, is decorated with a double row of pilasters of the Corinthian order, separated by a rich marble frieze and connected to the sides by volutes. The inside consists of a spacious nave covered with a barrel vault. In the church, there are no sacred furniture and there are only a few scraps of the frescoes that originally decorated each surface. Although annexed to the monastery, it is not part of the *Museo di Santa Giulia* and is used as a conference room.[[33]](#cite_note-monastic-33)

In the former vegetable garden of this monastery have been discovered a group of Roman [domus](/wiki/Domus "Domus") called *Domus dell'Ortaglia* that were used between the 1st and 4th centuries and they are some of the best preserved domus in northern Italy.

* *[Piazza della Loggia](/wiki/Piazza_della_Loggia "Piazza della Loggia")*, an example of [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") *piazza*, with the eponymous *Palazzo della [Loggia](/wiki/Loggia "Loggia")* (current Town Hall). Construction began in 1492 under the direction of Filippo de' Grassi and it was completed only in the 16th century by [Sansovino](/wiki/Jacopo_Sansovino "Jacopo Sansovino") and [Palladio](/wiki/Palladio "Palladio"). [Vanvitelli](/wiki/Luigi_Vanvitelli "Luigi Vanvitelli") designed the upper room of the palace (1769). On the south side of the square are two 15th–16th century *Monti di Pietà* (Christian lending houses). Their façades are embedded with ancient Roman tombstones, one of oldest antique lapidary displays in Italy.[[36]](#cite_note-36) At the centre of the east side of the square stands the *[Torre dell'Orologio](/wiki/Torre_dell%27Orologio,_Brescia "Torre dell'Orologio, Brescia")*, a tower with a large astronomical clock (mid-16th-century) on top of which there are two copper anthropomorphic automata which strike the hours on a bell. On 28 May 1974, the square was targeted by the [terrorist bombing](/wiki/Piazza_della_Loggia_bombing "Piazza della Loggia bombing").
* *[Duomo Vecchio](/wiki/Old_Cathedral,_Brescia "Old Cathedral, Brescia")*: the *Old Cathedral,* also known as *La Rotonda,* is a circular 11th-century [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") church. The main structure, with rustic exteriors, was built atop ruins of an earlier basilica. Near the entrance is the pink marble [sarcophagus of Berardo Maggi](/wiki/Sarcophagus_of_Berardo_Maggi "Sarcophagus of Berardo Maggi"), while in the presbytery is the entrance to the crypt of San Filastrio. The structure houses masterworks by [Alessandro Bonvicino](/wiki/Alessandro_Bonvicino "Alessandro Bonvicino") (*il Moretto*); [Girolamo Romanino](/wiki/Girolamo_Romanino "Girolamo Romanino"), [Palma il Giovane](/wiki/Palma_il_Giovane "Palma il Giovane"), [Francesco Maffei](/wiki/Francesco_Maffei "Francesco Maffei"), and others.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* *[Duomo Nuovo](/wiki/New_Cathedral,_Brescia "New Cathedral, Brescia")*: construction of the *New Cathedral* began in 1604 and it was only completed in 1825. Initially designed by Palladio, economic shortfalls led to younger local architects and artists completing initial work, including decorations by [Pietro Maria Bagnadore](/wiki/Pietro_Maria_Bagnadore "Pietro Maria Bagnadore"). The interior has major frescoes by [Il Moretto](/wiki/Il_Moretto "Il Moretto"). The high altarpiece is by [Jacopo Zoboli](/wiki/Jacopo_Zoboli "Jacopo Zoboli") (1735). The main attraction is the *Ark of Sts Apollonius and [Filastrius](/wiki/Filastrius "Filastrius")* (1510).[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* *[Broletto](/wiki/Broletto,_Brescia "Broletto, Brescia")*: the 12th-and 13th-century Town Hall, now houses offices of both the commune and province. On the Piazza front is the balcony from where the medieval city officials spoke to the townsfolk; on the north side, rises a tall tower called "Tower of Pégol" or "Tower of the People" (the [Lombard](/wiki/Eastern_Lombard "Eastern Lombard"): *Tòr del Pégol*), whose bells were once used to summon the citizens in moments of distress.
* *Piazza della Vittoria*, an example of Italian [Art Déco](/wiki/Art_D%C3%A9co "Art Déco") architecture. It was built between 1927 and 1932 by architect [Marcello Piacentini](/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini "Marcello Piacentini") through the demolition of part of the medieval old town and it has an L-shape. On the inside corner right there is the [Torrione INA](/wiki/Torrione_INA "Torrione INA"), the first skyscraper built in Italy.[[39]](#cite_note-sky-39) In the north background there is the large *Palazzo delle poste* ("Post Office building"), with its ocher-white two-tone upholstery. The *Torre della Rivoluzione* ("Tower of the Revolution") and three other buildings, recalling the classical architecture, complete the square.
* *Piazza del Foro*: site of the Roman forum. In addition to the already mentioned *Capitolium*, *republican sanctuary* and *Roman theatre*, various other remains are visible in the area. Among these, on the south side of the square, are scanty remains of a building called the *curia*, which may have been a basilica.
* *Palazzo Martinengo Cesaresco Novarino*: mid-17th-century palace, now home to art exhibitions and an underground archaeological exhibit, depicting city's history from the early [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") to the present day, concentrating in a single place 3,000 years of urban history of Brescia.[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* *[Santa Maria dei Miracoli](/wiki/Santa_Maria_dei_Miracoli_(Brescia) "Santa Maria dei Miracoli (Brescia)")*: (1488–1523) church with fine façade by [Giovanni Antonio Amadeo](/wiki/Giovanni_Antonio_Amadeo "Giovanni Antonio Amadeo"), decorated with bas-reliefs and a Renaissance *[peristilium](/wiki/Peristilium "Peristilium")*. It is considered a jewel of Renaissance sculpture in Lombardy.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* *[San Francesco](/wiki/San_Francesco,_Brescia "San Francesco, Brescia")*: Romanesque-Gothic church and cloisters.
* *Castle of Brescia*: also known as *Falcone d'Italia* ("Falcon of Italy"), it is located atop Cidneo Hill in the northeast corner of the town. The castle was built between the 13th and the 16th century, and it is among the largest castles in Italy.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Besides commanding a fine view of the city and a large part of the surrounding area, as well as being a local favorite recreational area, it hosts the Arms Museum, with a fine collection of weapons from the Middle Ages onwards; the *Risorgimento Museum*, dedicated to the Italian independence wars of the 19th century; an exhibition of model railroads; and an astronomical observatory.
* *[Santi Nazaro e Celso](/wiki/Santi_Nazaro_e_Celso_(Brescia) "Santi Nazaro e Celso (Brescia)")*: church housing the [Averoldi Polyptych](/wiki/Averoldi_Polyptych "Averoldi Polyptych") by [Titian](/wiki/Titian "Titian").
* *[San Faustino e Giovita](/wiki/Santi_Faustino_e_Giovita_(Brescia) "Santi Faustino e Giovita (Brescia)")*: church also known as *San Faustino Maggiore*. The interior has a fresco depicting *Apotheosis of Sts Faustino, Jovita, Benedict and Scholastica* by [Giandomenico Tiepolo](/wiki/Giandomenico_Tiepolo "Giandomenico Tiepolo").
* *[Basilica of Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie,_Brescia "Santa Maria delle Grazie, Brescia")*: basilica church built between the 16th and 17th centuries with Baroque frescoes and stucco, and a work of Il Moretto.
* *[San Giuseppe](/wiki/San_Giuseppe,_Brescia "San Giuseppe, Brescia")*: 16th-century church houses frescoes and decoration including fourteen *Stations of the Cross of St. Joseph* (1713) by [Giovanni Antonio Capello](/wiki/Giovanni_Antonio_Capello "Giovanni Antonio Capello"). The church houses the tombs of [Gasparo da Salò](/wiki/Gasparo_da_Sal%C3%B2 "Gasparo da Salò"), one of the inventors of the modern violin and [Benedetto Marcello](/wiki/Benedetto_Marcello "Benedetto Marcello"), Baroque musician. Inside it, there is one of the oldest [organs](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)") in the world.[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* *[San Clemente](/wiki/San_Clemente_(Brescia) "San Clemente (Brescia)")*: church with paintings by Bonvicino.
* *[Torre della Pallata](/wiki/Torre_della_Pallata,_Brescia "Torre della Pallata, Brescia")*: massive tower built in 1254 as part of the medieval walls. In the 15th century, the clock, merlons, and turret added. The fountain on the western side was designed in 1597 by Bagnadore.
* *San Giovanni*: church with a refectory painted jointly by il Moretto and il Romanino.
* *[San Marco Evangelista](/wiki/San_Marco_Evangelista,_Brescia "San Marco Evangelista, Brescia")*: a small 13th-century Romanesque-style church.
* *[San Mattia alle Grazie](/wiki/San_Mattia_alle_Grazie "San Mattia alle Grazie")*: a suppressed 13th-century former church.
* *[Monumental Cemetery](/wiki/Monumental_cemetery_of_Brescia "Monumental cemetery of Brescia")*: also known as *Vantiniano*, is the largest cemetery in Brescia, designed around 1813 by [Rodolfo Vantini](/wiki/Rodolfo_Vantini "Rodolfo Vantini"). It is the first monumental cemetery built in Italy[[44]](#cite_note-44) and at its centre stands the *Lighthouse of Brescia* (60 meters tall) which has inspired the architect [Heinrich Strack](/wiki/Heinrich_Strack "Heinrich Strack") for the design of the [Berlin Victory Column](/wiki/Berlin_Victory_Column "Berlin Victory Column").[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* *[Teatro Grande](/wiki/Teatro_Grande "Teatro Grande")*: opera house renovated several times between the mid-17th and mid-19th century. The name *Grande* ("Big") is derived from the former name *Il Grande* ("The Great") in honour of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte"). The horseshoe-shaped auditorium is richly decorated and has five galleries. Since 1912, the theatre is a [national monument](/wiki/National_monument "National monument").[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* *Biblioteca Queriniana*, containing rare early manuscripts, including the [Codex Brixianus](/wiki/Codex_Brixianus "Codex Brixianus"), a 14th-century manuscript of [Dante](/wiki/Dante "Dante"), and some rare [incunabula](/wiki/Incunabula "Incunabula").
* *[Brescia Due](/wiki/Brescia_Due "Brescia Due")*: a [business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") located in the southern part of the city.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-47)
* *[Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace,_Brescia "Crystal Palace, Brescia")*: as a part of Brescia Due, is the tallest habitable structure of the city with a height of 110 m (360 ft),[[47]](#cite_note-:0-47) it was built by the architect Bruno Fedrigolli between 1988 and 1992 and according to the first project this skyscraper would have been the tallest one in Italy.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The city has no fewer than seventy-two public fountains. The stone quarries of [Botticino](/wiki/Botticino "Botticino"), 8 km (5 mi) east of Brescia, supplied marble for the [Monument to Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Monument_to_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Monument to Vittorio Emanuele II") in Rome.

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Raffaello_Angelo_1_%28frammento_pala_Baronci%29.jpg/250px-Raffaello_Angelo_1_%28frammento_pala_Baronci%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raffaello_Angelo_1_(frammento_pala_Baronci).jpg)

Pinacoteca Tosio Martinengo: *Angel* by [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael")

The most important museums of Brescia are the following:

* *[Museo di Santa Giulia](/wiki/Museo_di_Santa_Giulia "Museo di Santa Giulia")* ("Santa Giulia Museum"): it is the city Museum, situated in the monastic complex of San Salvatore-Santa Giulia, which has a rich Roman section. One of the masterpieces is the bronze statue of a winged Victory, originally probably a Venus, converted in antiquity into the Victory by adding the wings; it is said to be in the act of writing the winner's name on her shield (now lost). Also very interesting, one of the very few places in the world where the remains of two Roman domus can be visited on their original site simply by strolling into one of the museum halls.
* *[Pinacoteca Tosio Martinengo](/wiki/Pinacoteca_Tosio_Martinengo "Pinacoteca Tosio Martinengo")*, the municipal art gallery; it hosts works of the painters of the Renaissance Brescian school, [Girolamo Romanino](/wiki/Girolamo_Romanino "Girolamo Romanino"), [Alessandro Bonvicino](/wiki/Alessandro_Bonvicino "Alessandro Bonvicino") and [Giovanni Battista Moroni](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Moroni "Giovanni Battista Moroni"). After an extensive remodeling the museum reopened in 2018 with a refreshed interior showcasing the art hung on contemporary fabric covered walls.
* *Museo della Mille Miglia* ("Mille Miglia Museum"). Situated inside the former Monastery of S. Eufemia, the museum celebrates the history of the 1,600-kilometre (1,000 mi) car race from Brescia to Rome and back that began in 1927. It shows films, memorabilia, dresses, posters, and a number of classic cars that are periodically replaced by other in case of participation in events.[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* *Museo Diocesano di Brescia* ("[Diocesan Museum of Brescia](/wiki/Diocesan_museum_of_Brescia,_Italy "Diocesan museum of Brescia, Italy")"). It is located in the former Monastery of St. Joseph and houses a permanent collection of sacred artworks, including paintings, [illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript"), as well as one of the most extensive collections of [vestments](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment") in Italy.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* *Museo Nazionale della fotografia* ("National Museum of Photography"). It hosts a collection of photographic and cinematographic machines, along with various camera accessories and a photo library with about 60,000 photographs.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
* *Museo delle Armi* "Luigi Marzoli" ("Luigi Marzoli" Arms Museum"). Located in the Castle, it is one of the most important European collections of old armour and weaponry. It hosts about 600 pieces of armour, weapons and firearms from the 15th to the 19th century.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
* *Museo degli strumenti musicali e della liuteria bresciana* ("Museum of the Musical Instruments and Brescian lutherie"). It hosts string and wind instruments, as well as a rich collection of [choirbooks](/wiki/Choirbook "Choirbook") and musical scores.
* *Collezione Paolo VI – arte contemporanea* ("Paul VI Collection – Contemporary Art"). It is located in [Concesio](/wiki/Concesio "Concesio"), on the northern outskirts of Brescia, and hosts the contemporary art collection of [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI"), composed of about 7,000 works of many famous artists, including [Matisse](/wiki/Matisse "Matisse"), [Chagall](/wiki/Chagall "Chagall"), [Picasso](/wiki/Picasso "Picasso"), [Dalí](/wiki/Dal%C3%AD "Dalí") and others. It was opened on 8 November 2009, inaugurated by [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Parco_delle_Cave_cava_Case_San_Polo_Brescia.jpg/500px-Parco_delle_Cave_cava_Case_San_Polo_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parco_delle_Cave_cava_Case_San_Polo_Brescia.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Casa_Circondariale_Nerio_Fischione_e_Parco_Mazzolari_Brescia.jpg/500px-Casa_Circondariale_Nerio_Fischione_e_Parco_Mazzolari_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_Circondariale_Nerio_Fischione_e_Parco_Mazzolari_Brescia.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Snow_%2848426354%29.jpeg/330px-Snow_%2848426354%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Snow_(48426354).jpeg)

**From left to right**: *Parco delle Cave*, *Parco Mazzolari* and *Parco Ducos* in winter

Due to its location in the foothills of the Alps, Brescia has forests close to the city centre. About 80% of its municipal territory is covered by woodlands and farmlands: total amount of public green space is 26.3 square kilometres (10.2 sq mi), or 134 square metres (1,440 sq ft) per inhabitant, while agricultural zones cover an area of 45.6 square kilometres (17.6 sq mi).[[54]](#cite_note-park-54)

The largest park of Brescia is *Parco delle Colline di Brescia* ("Brescia Hills Park") that has a total surface of 43.09 square kilometres (16.64 mi2),[[55]](#cite_note-55) of which 21.83 square kilometres (8.43 mi2) fall within the city limits.[[54]](#cite_note-park-54) The park was established in 2000 with the purpose of preserving, safeguarding, and enhancing the natural heritage of the hills surrounding Brescia. Woods cover about 70% of the surface of the park; the rest consists of meadows, vineyard and olive plantations. The most common plants in the park are [hop-hornbeam](/wiki/Ostrya_carpinifolia "Ostrya carpinifolia"), [downy oak](/wiki/Downy_oak "Downy oak"), [sweet chestnut](/wiki/Sweet_chestnut "Sweet chestnut"), [manna ash](/wiki/Manna_ash "Manna ash"), but there is also the presence of Mediterranean species such as [terebinth](/wiki/Pistacia_terebinthus "Pistacia terebinthus"), [tree heath](/wiki/Tree_heath "Tree heath"), [bay laurel](/wiki/Bay_laurel "Bay laurel") and [holm oak](/wiki/Quercus_ilex "Quercus ilex"). The fauna of the park includes foxes, [European badgers](/wiki/European_badger "European badger"), [wild boars](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar") and other mammals, while the most common birds are [robins](/wiki/European_robin "European robin"), [blackbirds](/wiki/Common_blackbird "Common blackbird"), [blackcaps](/wiki/Blackcap "Blackcap") and [wrens](/wiki/Eurasian_wren "Eurasian wren").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 2018 another public park known as *Parco delle Cave* was opened on the site of former sand quarries in the south of the city. After the full opening at the end of 2021, now the park covers an area of 2 km2 (490 acres).

Other parks are scattered throughout the city, such as *Parco del Castello* ("Castle Park"), *Parco Tarello*, *Parco Mazzolari*, *Parco Ducos* and *Campo di Marte*.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Santa_Chiara_-_Facolt%C3%A0_di_Economia_-_Brescia_%28Foto_Luca_Giarelli%29.jpg/250px-Santa_Chiara_-_Facolt%C3%A0_di_Economia_-_Brescia_%28Foto_Luca_Giarelli%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Chiara_-_Facolt%C3%A0_di_Economia_-_Brescia_(Foto_Luca_Giarelli).jpg)

University of Brescia, Economics faculty

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Facciata_del_Liceo_Arnaldo_Brescia.jpg/250px-Facciata_del_Liceo_Arnaldo_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Facciata_del_Liceo_Arnaldo_Brescia.jpg)

[Classic lyceum](/wiki/Liceo_classico "Liceo classico") "[Arnaldo](/wiki/Arnold_of_Brescia "Arnold of Brescia")", established in 1797, is one of the oldest and most prominent high schools in Brescia.

As 2019, in Brescia there are 51 primary schools, of which 42 public and 9 private. There are also 29 [lower secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_Italy "Secondary education in Italy"), of which 21 public and 8 private.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Referring to [upper secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_Italy "Secondary education in Italy"), in Brescia there are 53 schools, of which 20 are private and 33 are public. Among them there are 3 [classic lyceums](/wiki/Liceo_classico "Liceo classico") and 13 [scientific lyceums](/wiki/Liceo_scientifico "Liceo scientifico").

Brescia has two universities:

* [University of Brescia](/wiki/University_of_Brescia "University of Brescia") is a public university founded in 1982 and ranked among the Top 700 universities worldwide.[[58]](#cite_note-58) It is divided into 4 faculties: Economics, Engineering, Law, Medicine and Surgery.
* Catholic University of Brescia, founded in 1968, is a satellite campus of the [Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore](/wiki/Universit%C3%A0_Cattolica_del_Sacro_Cuore "Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore"). It is divided into 6 faculties: Literature and Philosophy; Psychology; Education; Language Sciences and Foreign Literature; Mathematics, Physics and Natural Sciences; Political and Social Sciences.

Brescia is also home of two academies of fine art (*[Libera Accademia di Belle Arti](/wiki/Libera_Accademia_di_Belle_Arti "Libera Accademia di Belle Arti")* (LABA) and *Accademia di Belle Arti SantaGiulia*) and a conservatory of music (*Conservatorio Luca Marenzio*).

## Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Brescia is an important medical centre. The main hospital of the city is *Spedali Civili di Brescia*, which has 2,180 beds and an employed staff of 6,175.[[59]](#cite_note-59) It was founded in 1427 and is considered the second best hospital in Italy.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Other hospitals are located in the city: *Fondazione Poliambulanza*, *Casa di Cura S. Camillo*, *Istituto Clinico S. Anna* and *Istituto Clinico Città di Brescia*.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Economy")]

The city is at the centre of the third largest Italian industrial area.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The local [Confindustria](/wiki/Confindustria "Confindustria"), the AIB – Associazione Industriale Bresciana (Industrial Association of Brescia), was the first industry association founded in Italy in 1897.[[62]](#cite_note-62) The Brescian companies are typically a small or medium-sized, often family-run, ranging from the food to the engineering industry.

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Vigneto_Pusterla_Ronco_Capretti_e_Ronchi_Brescia.jpg/250px-Vigneto_Pusterla_Ronco_Capretti_e_Ronchi_Brescia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vigneto_Pusterla_Ronco_Capretti_e_Ronchi_Brescia.jpg)

Vineyards in the middle of the city with an extension of 4 ha (9.9 acres)

The [viticulture](/wiki/Viticulture "Viticulture") is the most important agricultural sector of the Brescian [food system](/wiki/Food_system "Food system"). The municipality of Brescia is part of the production areas of five different wines: a [DOCG](/wiki/DOCG "DOCG") wine, i.e. the *[Franciacorta](/wiki/Franciacorta_DOCG "Franciacorta DOCG")*,[[63]](#cite_note-63) three [DOC](/wiki/Denominazione_di_origine_controllata "Denominazione di origine controllata") wines (*Botticino*,[[64]](#cite_note-64) *Cellatica*[[65]](#cite_note-65) and *Curtefranca*[[66]](#cite_note-66)) and an [IGT](/wiki/Indicazione_geografica_tipica "Indicazione geografica tipica") wine (*Ronchi di Brescia*[[67]](#cite_note-67)). In addition, in its old town, along the northern slope of the Cidneo Hill, there is the largest urban [vineyard](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard") in Europe,[[68]](#cite_note-68) characterized by the cultivation of *Invernenga*, a local white grape variety present in Brescia since Roman times.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Another very important sector is the production of [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), especially in the nearby area of Lake Garda. The European Union has recorded as [PDO](/wiki/Geographical_indications_and_traditional_specialities_in_the_European_Union "Geographical indications and traditional specialities in the European Union") two typologies of extra virgin olive oils and they are *Garda* and *Laghi lombardi*.

Brescia is also the homeland of Italian [caviar](/wiki/Caviar "Caviar"). In [Calvisano](/wiki/Calvisano "Calvisano"), about 30 kilometres (19 mi) south of the city centre, is located the world's largest sturgeons farm[[70]](#cite_note-70) that produces annually 25 tonnes (25 long tons; 28 short tons) of caviar exported all over the world.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Industry and services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Industry and services")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Rascacielos_Crystal_Palace_Brescia.JPG/250px-Rascacielos_Crystal_Palace_Brescia.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rascacielos_Crystal_Palace_Brescia.JPG)

[The business district of Brescia](/wiki/Brescia_Due "Brescia Due")

The main industrial activities of Brescia are those mechanical, specialized in the production and distribution of machine tools. Also important is the production of [motor vehicle](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle"), represented by the [OM](/wiki/Officine_Meccaniche "Officine Meccaniche"), which is the manufacturer of [Iveco](/wiki/Iveco "Iveco") trucks, and the production of [weapons](/wiki/Weapons "Weapons"), among which the Fausti, [Beretta](/wiki/Beretta "Beretta"), Fabarm and [Perazzi](/wiki/Perazzi "Perazzi"). Very important is the metallurgical industry. On the outskirts of town, there are two steel mills: the "Alfa Acciai" and "Ori Martin". Other crucial industrial activities are the production of [cutlery](/wiki/Cutlery "Cutlery") and [faucets](/wiki/Faucets "Faucets"), along with the textile, footwear and clothing, as well as the production of [building materials](/wiki/Building_materials "Building materials") and [bricks](/wiki/Bricks "Bricks"). The intense industrial development has resulted in a high level of pollution in the outskirts of the city located near the disused chemical factory "Caffaro" that produced PCB. For this reason, this part of the city is in the list of SIN – Siti di Interesse Nazionale (Sites of National Interest). According to a study carried out by the Edison Foundation and [Confindustria](/wiki/General_Confederation_of_Italian_Industry "General Confederation of Italian Industry") in 2015, Brescia is the province with the highest value added by industry in Europe.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Brescia hosts the headquarters of several industry groups, including the [Lucchini](/wiki/Gruppo_Lucchini "Gruppo Lucchini") Group, the Feralpi and the Camozzi Group. Brescia is also home to the [A2A](/wiki/A2A "A2A") Group (the result of the merger of ASM Brescia, AEM Milano and AMSA).

The financial sector is also a major employer, with the presence of several branches of banks and financial assets. The [UBI Banca](/wiki/UBI_Banca "UBI Banca") Group, fourth largest banking group in Italy, has several division headquarters in the city.

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Contrada_Pozzo_dell%27Olmo_bRESCIA.jpg/250px-Contrada_Pozzo_dell%27Olmo_bRESCIA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Contrada_Pozzo_dell%27Olmo_bRESCIA.jpg)

A street in the old town

The significant historical and artistic heritage of Brescia (since 2011 in the UNESCO World Heritage list) and the natural beauties of its surrounding area (like the [Lake Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda"), the [Val Camonica](/wiki/Val_Camonica "Val Camonica") and the [Lake Iseo](/wiki/Lake_Iseo "Lake Iseo")) have allowed the city to attract an increasing number of visitors. In 10 years, the number of tourists who visited Brescia has almost doubled from 142,556 in 2003[[73]](#cite_note-73) to over 280,000 in 2013.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Additionally, Brescia is close to important tourist destinations ([Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") can be directly reached in 45 minutes by train, [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") and [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") in about 2 hours) and is one of the cheapest cities in Italy in terms of hotel stays.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) For these reasons, tourists often use Brescia as a base to explore the surrounding places.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Transport")]

Brescia Mobilità (BM) is the statutory corporation responsible for the transport network in Brescia; it operates one metro line (Brescia Metro) and 19 urban bus lines. Besides public transport, BM manages the interchange parking lots and other transportation services including [bike sharing](/wiki/Bike_sharing "Bike sharing") and [carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") systems.

Since 2004 in the city center of Brescia is active a traffic restricted zone or ZTL ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Zona a Traffico Limitato*). The objective of the ZTL, together with a program of pedestrianizations of the main squares and streets of the historical center, is to drastically reduce the chronic traffic jams that take place in the city of Brescia, promoting [sustainable mobility](/wiki/Sustainable_mobility "Sustainable mobility") and public transport, and decreasing the existing levels of smog that have become unsustainable from the point of view of public health.

### Brescia Metro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Brescia Metro")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Metropolitana_di_Brescia_-_Fermata_Vittoria_-_banchina.JPG/250px-Metropolitana_di_Brescia_-_Fermata_Vittoria_-_banchina.JPG)](/wiki/File:Metropolitana_di_Brescia_-_Fermata_Vittoria_-_banchina.JPG)

A station of [Brescia Metro](/wiki/Brescia_Metro "Brescia Metro")

The [Brescia Metro](/wiki/Brescia_Metro "Brescia Metro") is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") network that opened on 2 March 2013.[[78]](#cite_note-cityrailways.it-78) The network comprises one line, 13.7 kilometres (9 mi) long,[[79]](#cite_note-mappe-79) with 17 stations[[79]](#cite_note-mappe-79) between *Buffalora* and *Prealpino*, of which 13 are underground.

The first projects for a metro in Brescia date back to the 1980s, with the introduction of the first fully automatic light metro systems in other mid-size cities in Europe.
Two feasibility studies were commissioned in 1987. The automatic light metro system was chosen as the best technology for the city. The first public tender was announced in 1989. But this project was then cancelled in 1996.

In 1994, the first application for public financing was issued. The public financing form the central government arrived in 1995, while other funds arrived in 2002 from the [Region](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"). The international public bid for the first phase of the project was announced in 2000. The winning proposal was from a group of companies comprising [Ansaldo STS](/wiki/Ansaldo_STS "Ansaldo STS"), [AnsaldoBreda](/wiki/AnsaldoBreda "AnsaldoBreda"), [Astaldi](/wiki/Astaldi "Astaldi") and [Acciona](/wiki/Acciona "Acciona"), with a system similar to that of the Copenhagen metro.

A €575 million contract was awarded to a consortium led by [Ansaldo STS](/wiki/Ansaldo_STS "Ansaldo STS") in April 2003.[[80]](#cite_note-rgi200804-80) Work started in January 2004, but archaeological finds caused delays and required station redesigns.[[78]](#cite_note-cityrailways.it-78)[[81]](#cite_note-opening-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Mappa_tram_Brescia_1914.svg/250px-Mappa_tram_Brescia_1914.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mappa_tram_Brescia_1914.svg)

The construction of the new light rail line, from the quarter of Pendolina to city fair center, was funded by [Italian Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport](/wiki/Ministry_of_Infrastructure_and_Transport_(Italy) "Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport (Italy)") for 422 milion euros.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The city council approved the definitive project on 24 June 2024.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Stazione_ferroviaria_di_Brescia.JPG/250px-Stazione_ferroviaria_di_Brescia.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stazione_ferroviaria_di_Brescia.JPG)

The train station of Brescia

Brescia has four railway stations. The [main station](/wiki/Brescia_railway_station "Brescia railway station"), which opened in 1854, is located on the [Milan-Venice railway](/wiki/Milan-Venice_railway "Milan-Venice railway") and is the starting point for the [Brescia-Iseo-Edolo](/wiki/Brescia%E2%80%93Iseo%E2%80%93Edolo_railway "Brescia–Iseo–Edolo railway"), [Brescia-Cremona](/wiki/Brescia%E2%80%93Cremona_railway "Brescia–Cremona railway"), [Brescia-Parma](/wiki/Brescia%E2%80%93Parma_railway "Brescia–Parma railway") and [Bergamo–Brescia](/wiki/Bergamo%E2%80%93Brescia_railway "Bergamo–Brescia railway") rail lines. The station has 15 platforms and is used by about 20 million passengers per year. Other railway stations are *[Brescia Borgo San Giovanni](/wiki/Brescia_Borgo_San_Giovanni_railway_station "Brescia Borgo San Giovanni railway station")* and *[Brescia Violino](/wiki/Brescia_Violino_railway_station "Brescia Violino railway station")* (two lesser stations that are located on the Brescia-Iseo-Edolo railway) and *Brescia Scalo*, with no passenger service and used as a freight station.

From Brescia, [high speed trains](/wiki/High_speed_trains "High speed trains") connect to [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Rome, [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") and [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"); one can reach Milan in 35 min, Venice in 1h and 35 min, Florence in 2 hours and 15 min and Rome in 3 hours and 35 min. In addition there are international day trains to Zurich, and overnight sleeper services to Paris and Dijon (Thello), Munich and Vienna (ÖBB).

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Roads")]

Brescia is connected with the rest of northern Italy by three motorways:

* [A4](/wiki/Autostrada_A4_(Italy) "Autostrada A4 (Italy)"), that is the main axis connecting the city with the east and the west of the country, to cities such as Milan, Turin, Venice and Trieste;
* [A21](/wiki/Autostrada_A21_(Italy) "Autostrada A21 (Italy)"), which connects Brescia to Turin with a more southern route than A4;

## Pollution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Pollution")]

Brescia is at the top of the ranking of European cities with the highest preventable mortality burdens for PM2.5 pollution in a new study published in January 2021 by The Lancet Planetary Health,[[84]](#cite_note-84) which estimates the death rate associated with fine [particulate matter](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates") (PM2.5) and [nitrogen dioxide](/wiki/Nitrogen_dioxide "Nitrogen dioxide") (NO2) pollution in 1000 European cities.

Legambiente based on the number of days the legal air-quality limits were breached in 2018. The report said Brescia failed to respect the legal limits for 150 days last year, 103 for ozone and 47 for Pm10 particles.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Mille_Miglia_Museum.jpg/250px-Mille_Miglia_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mille_Miglia_Museum.jpg)

Mille Miglia Museum

Brescia was the starting and end point of the historical car race [Mille Miglia](/wiki/Mille_Miglia "Mille Miglia") that took place annually in May until 1957 on a Brescia-Rome-Brescia itinerary, and also the now defunct [Coppa Florio](/wiki/Coppa_Florio "Coppa Florio"), one of the first ever sport motor races. The Mille Miglia tradition is now kept alive by the "Historic Mille Miglia",[[86]](#cite_note-86) a world-class event that gathers in Brescia every year thousands of fans of motor sports and of vintage sports cars. The only cars admitted to the race are the ones that could have competed in (although they do not necessarily have to have taken part in) the original Mille Miglia. The race nowadays is not however a speed race anymore, but rather a "regularity" race; speed races have actually been banned on regular [roads in Italy](/wiki/Roads_in_Italy "Roads in Italy") because of the deadly accident that killed a driver and ten bystanders in the last minutes of the 1957 Mille Miglia – that therefore became the last of the original races.  
In recent years, many celebrities have participated in the Mille Miglia, including [Rowan Atkinson](/wiki/Rowan_Atkinson "Rowan Atkinson"), [Daniel Day-Lewis](/wiki/Daniel_Day-Lewis "Daniel Day-Lewis"), [Jeremy Irons](/wiki/Jeremy_Irons "Jeremy Irons"), [Jay Leno](/wiki/Jay_Leno "Jay Leno"), [Brian Johnson](/wiki/Brian_Johnson "Brian Johnson"), [Elliot Gleave](/wiki/Elliot_Gleave "Elliot Gleave"), [David Gandy](/wiki/David_Gandy "David Gandy"), [Jodie Kidd](/wiki/Jodie_Kidd "Jodie Kidd"), [Yasmin Le Bon](/wiki/Yasmin_Le_Bon "Yasmin Le Bon") and others.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Brescia is also the home of the [Brescia Calcio](/wiki/Brescia_Calcio "Brescia Calcio") football club and the [Rugby Leonessa 1928](/wiki/Rugby_Leonessa_1928 "Rugby Leonessa 1928").

Since 1984, the Schermabrescia fencing club is active. Brescia born foil-fencer [Andrea Cassarà](/wiki/Andrea_Cassar%C3%A0 "Andrea Cassarà") won the gold medal at the [2011 World Fencing Championships](/wiki/2011_World_Fencing_Championships "2011 World Fencing Championships").

Brescia is the home of the [Basket Brescia Leonessa](/wiki/Basket_Brescia_Leonessa "Basket Brescia Leonessa") basketball club. Leonessa has its home arena in the new [PalaLeonessa](/wiki/PalaLeonessa "PalaLeonessa"),[[90]](#cite_note-90) inaugurated in 2018, with a capacity of 5,200.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

## People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: People")]

The monument representing a lion, the sign on the coat of arms of the city. ### Fountains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brescia&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Fountains")]

For many years Brescia has been considered a "city of water" due to the presence of many canals and natural waterways, as the French author [Paul de Musset](/wiki/Paul_de_Musset "Paul de Musset") (1804–1880) once wrote: "The wide streets and numerous fountains give it an air of a big city. Water gushes in the squares and circulates in private homes almost as abundantly as in Rome".[[99]](#cite_note-99)
